title: TBM 401: Solving Problems the Hard Way
questions: How do we define the right number of work lanes (not too vague, not too specific) and assign real ownership?
What routines help teams keep tempo, habits, and focus without over-correcting when drift appears?
How can leaders avoid alignment theater and zombie processes while still using lightweight planning artifacts?
How should lanes change over time—when to refactor, pivot, or retire them?
summary: This post argues that effective leadership and strategy execution come from shaping a small set of durable “lanes” with clear ownership, weekly calibration, and habit/tempo coaching—because drift and uncertainty are inevitable. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who want practical operating routines that avoid alignment theater and instead build real learning through collaborative discovery and ongoing refactoring. The piece also critiques overly rigid, frameworkified versions of agile and reframes planning as a continuous sensemaking practice across organizational levels.

title: TBM 325: Skeptoptimism – Thinking Slow, Acting Fast
questions: How do I think slow without falling into analysis paralysis?
What does it mean to plan through experimentation rather than abstract calculation?
How can I communicate skepticism and optimism so others don’t misread deep dives as pessimism or delay?
How do I ensure my team is truly aligned before I rely on their ability to pivot and recover?
summary: The post argues that “thinking slow, acting fast” should be practiced as active experimentation and experience, not passive deliberation. It’s aimed at leaders and team members who identify as skeptoptimists and want to navigate the tension between deep risk exploration and rapid execution, including how to communicate optimism so others don’t mistake it for waste or pessimism.

title: TBM 280: 2-6-4-1
questions: How can we structure a quarter so we don’t waste time prepping for the next quarter and then reset two weeks in?
What sprint or release cadence helps teams ship to customers and still have time to iterate based on feedback?
How do we reduce end-of-quarter rush and context switching while protecting deep work?
What does a practical full-cycle learning-build-measure loop look like over a quarter?
summary: This post proposes a timeboxed way to run a quarter—2 weeks of full-team research and discovery, 6 weeks to ship a production-ready customer-facing release, 4 weeks to iterate with feedback and analytics, and 1 week to close out loose ends. It’s aimed at product and engineering teams that struggle with end-of-quarter rushing, non-inclusive planning, and insufficient time to learn and iterate. The guidance emphasizes clearing the decks, limiting interruptions, and treating the cadence as an experiment with an expiration date.

title: TBM 259: The Predictability Trap
questions: Should predictable delivery be the goal or a supporting metric for value creation?
How do you achieve predictable innovation without turning delivery into commitment theater?
What operational levers actually improve delivery predictability beyond better estimates?
What happens when a company demands predictability while teams carry accumulated debt and cannot slow down?
search_terms: lean flow levers WIP batch size
summary: The post argues that “predictable delivery” should serve value creation and sustainable differentiated growth, not replace them as the primary objective. It contrasts businesses that depend on predictable feature delivery (and therefore obsess over commitments) with teams that need predictable innovation by managing complexity and trimming low-value work. It also claims that delivery predictability is driven more by flow and execution practices than by better estimates, and that demanding predictability while carrying accumulated debt can eventually make it unattainable.

title: TBM 228: What Slows Us Down
questions: Why do companies that value speed still end up moving slowly?
What causes teams to keep shipping quickly until things start to drag?
Which mental models of product development lead to bloat and accumulated debt?
How can a business model enable sustainable velocity rather than temporary momentum?
summary: The post argues that organizational speed is constrained by incentives and psychology, especially survival pressure and success-driven inertia that can turn into gluttony. It is aimed at product and engineering leaders who want to diagnose why teams slow down over time and how flawed development metaphors undermine sustainable velocity. The author emphasizes that aligning business model, quality, customer value, and iterative collaboration is key, and that mental models typically change only through lived experience.

title: TBM 201: The Myth of Quality Tradeoffs
questions: Is quality really something you have to trade off against speed and innovation?
Why do some organizations keep quality relatively high under the same delivery pressures?
How can teams ship fast while still improving quality over time?
What happens when an organization never explicitly defines quality?
search_terms: accidental quality drift vs deliberate tradeoffs
summary: This post challenges the common narrative that teams must balance quality against speed by reframing many “tradeoff” stories as post-hoc rationalizations for quality drift under uncertainty and short-term pressure. It’s aimed at product and engineering leaders and practitioners who want ways to achieve innovation and fast delivery without sacrificing quality, emphasizing shared quality definitions, patience, skills, organizational design, and leadership beliefs.

title: TBM 51/52: Shaky Foundations (and Planning Escalation)
questions: How do we break a cycle of churn, pivots, and reactivity in product execution?
Why do big-bang plans fail when the organization lacks a strong foundation?
What does it mean to operate in exploration mode without stalling progress?
How should startups and larger companies structure strategy and ways of working as layered foundations?
summary: This post argues that persistent churn and pivoting indicate a weak organizational foundation, which leads teams to manufacture certainty via big-bang plans or costly exploration. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to stabilize execution by slowing down, building layered foundations (strategy and ways of working), and shifting to smaller “think big, work small” efforts that enable learning. It also explains how these dynamics create predictable stress-driven camps and burnout cycles.

title: TBM 14/52: Less Mono-Process (VIDEO)
questions: How do teams end up using the same process for every type of work?
What are the risks of a mono-process approach?
How can teams choose different processes for different work types?
search_terms: one process for all work problems
summary: The post highlights how teams can fall into the “mono-process” trap—using one standard way of working for all types of tasks. It’s aimed at team leads and practitioners who want to improve delivery by tailoring processes to the nature of the work.

title: TBM 1/52: Rough Mixes
questions: How can teams use integration points to drive creativity instead of process compliance?
What does “making something that works” look like in an agile sprint?
How do feedback and repeated demo iterations help converge on a finished product?
What real-world test methods can replace purely internal review cycles?
search_terms: music production metaphor for product teams
summary: The post uses teenage music “rough mixes” and car-based listening rituals as a metaphor for how teams should build products: integrate early, test in the real world, and iterate via feedback. It argues that sprint frameworks often miss this purpose by overemphasizing planning mechanics instead of the integration forcing function that drives improvement. It’s aimed at product and engineering teams looking to make agile execution more creative and outcome-focused.

title: TBM 4/52: Think Big, Work Small (Part 2)
questions: How does my team work today—big, small, or big-defined with small execution?
What incentives are keeping us stuck in a dysfunctional delivery pattern?
How can we align on a mission while still leaving room for learning and feedback?
Where should we start: smaller iterations, a clearer big-picture mission, or less prescriptive planning?
summary: This post contrasts common team operating modes—working big, working small, and hybrid approaches—and explains why each can produce predictable dysfunction. It argues for a “thinking big, working small” model that couples a coherent, outcome-oriented mission with learning-first, broad-stroke execution. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to improve delivery effectiveness and break incentive-driven cycles.

title: TBM 46/53: Think Big, Work Small
questions: How can a team support both big-picture thinking and small iterative delivery?
What causes teams to iterate to nowhere instead of making progress toward a coherent strategy?
Why do some organizations diminish strategy or working small, and how can they correct that?
How do you avoid splitting the organization into “think big” and “work small” groups?
search_terms: learning as fast as you ship
agile used only to ship faster
summary: The post argues that effective product teams must do both big-picture strategy and small iterative execution, using thin slices to de-risk and learn as they ship. It explains why teams often over-index on one side—either dismissing strategy or treating agile as mere faster shipping—and warns against organizing people into separate “think big” vs “work small” groups. It’s aimed at product leaders and teams trying to improve how they plan, iterate, and create tangible progress together.

title: TBM 29/53: Shipping Faster Than You Learn (or…)
questions: Are we shipping faster than we learn, or learning faster than we ship?
What is causing our product complexity to clog the flow of work and slow learning?
How do we prevent customer-feedback-driven work from becoming generally good but not transformative?
Which forcing functions and learning practices should we institutionalize to balance shipping and learning?
search_terms: product development feedback loop vs factory line
forcing functions for learning and shipping
weekly usability testing and customer interviews
summary: This post argues that product teams often fall into an imbalance where shipping outpaces learning (or learning piles up due to complexity), leading to frustration and incremental outcomes. It’s aimed at product, design, and product analytics practitioners who want practical ways to visualize the work system as a loop and add forcing functions—like learning reviews, exploratory research, usability testing, and kill-a-feature days—to build both learning and shipping capabilities.

title: TBM 28/53: Developer & Designer Collaboration (in the Real World)
questions: How should developers and designers collaborate when work isn’t predictable?
What does an effort need right now from each teammate?
How do we balance sprint/Agile pressure with designers’ need for deep work?
How can teams adapt their working agreements instead of following “The One Way”?
summary: The post uses a real-world developer-designer collaboration example to argue that effective teamwork often involves alternating individual and collaborative work, with divergence followed by frequent integration. It’s aimed at product and delivery teams (and their managers) who feel pressured to follow rigid Agile/UX “rules,” encouraging them to adapt working agreements to the specific needs of the effort and teammates.

title: TBM 16/53: The Boring Bits
questions: How can a product team get buy-in for the unglamorous process work that enables delivery?
What should teams do when learning reviews or learning backlogs feel awkward or inefficient at first?
How do you design and run process experiments without overwhelming the team?
How do you build trust so people give new practices a fair trial before discarding them?
search_terms: pre-read and meeting notes best practices
summary: The post argues that high-performing product teams depend on repetitive, unglamorous meta-work (pre-reads, notes, recaps, learning/decision reviews, and learning backlogs) that is often dismissed as bureaucracy. It’s aimed at product and agile leaders who need a practical way to build team trust and support for these practices by running them as time-bound experiments, expecting an initial dip, and adapting based on feedback.

title: TBM 9/53: Some MVP and Experiment Tips
questions: How do we run many experiments without overwhelming the team or creating too much work in progress?
What makes an MVP “good” for learning rather than a commitment or dependency?
How should we handle safety, blast radius, and communication when experiments can impact others?
When and how should we scale a successful experiment up and out across teams?
search_terms: how to run experiments on a team
summary: This post offers practical guidance for teams running lots of MVPs and experiments, especially when they struggle to keep the work manageable and learning-focused. It emphasizes small, killable bets with clear measurement and pivot/proceed criteria, plus team practices like limiting work in progress, partnering for de-biasing, and communicating blast radius. It’s aimed at product and engineering teams seeking higher experimentation cadence and safer scaling of what works.
